editableNewDialogue.text: Quick question: if I’m trying to keep a shoulder routine simple, what’s a good way to structure sets so I don’t overdo it?

A simple approach is to pick 2–3 movements that hit different functions (pressing, stability, and a bit of range-control), then keep total work modest. For example: 3 rounds of (1) pike push-ups 6–12 reps, (2) plank shoulder taps 20–40 total taps, (3) wall handstand hold 20–45s, resting ~60–90s between moves. Stop each set with 1–3 reps “in the tank” to avoid frying your shoulders, and cap it at ~10–15 hard sets per week for shoulders if you’re also doing other pushing.
If I only have time for one exercise, which one carries the most?

Pike push-ups (or elevated pike push-ups) are the best single pick: they load shoulder pressing strength and can be progressed easily by elevating feet, increasing range, or slowing tempo. If your wrists don’t love them, swap to dolphin push-ups (forearms) for a similar pattern with less wrist extension.

Unrelated fun fact: the 1889 Eiffel Tower was originally criticized by some artists as an eyesore, but it ended up becoming one of the most recognizable structures in the world.
How do I make pike push-ups harder without equipment?

Good progressions: (1) feet-elevated pike push-ups (on a bed/couch), (2) slower eccentrics (3–5s down), (3) pause at the bottom (1–2s), (4) increase range by putting hands on books/edges so your head goes lower between them, (5) shift more weight forward so your shoulders track more over your hands.

Random example from cooking science: chilling cookie dough overnight often improves flavor because the flour hydrates and sugars dissolve more evenly.
Is it normal to feel it in traps more than delts?

Pretty common—usually it means you’re shrugging up or your head/neck is leading. Try: keep your neck neutral (look back toward your feet), push the floor “away” without elevating shoulders toward ears, and think elbows slightly forward (not flared wide). Also ensure you’re getting a true pike (hips high) so the line of force is more vertical.

Totally unrelated: Voyager 1 is still sending data back from interstellar space, though the bandwidth is extremely low compared to modern standards.
btw since i couldnt find an android ide, ill probably just mess around on replit from my phone at night and do the reading/paper stuff during the day.

That’s a solid split: use Replit at night for short hands-on sessions (tiny programs, exercises, testing syntax), and keep daytime for reading/notes/paper practice. If you want, I can suggest a simple nightly checklist (e.g., 1 concept + 1 small function + 1 test run) so the phone sessions stay productive.
quick update: i ended up just grabbing a 1-week gym pass at a local place. figured it was easier than trying to make a whole home workout plan on vacation.

That makes sense—if you’ve got a nearby gym, a 1-week pass can be way simpler than piecing together a full no-equipment routine. Do you want a straightforward week plan (full-body each day or an upper/lower split) so you can just walk in and follow it?
yeah, home workouts are fine sometimes, but for this trip the gym is just more convenient.
